# Source Record
- Page title: `nbadb — NBA Data Warehouse`
- Site framing: public landing page for the hosted docs experience.
- Public product language centers on four promises: star schema, endpoint coverage, lineage, and analyst-ready access paths.
- Prominent routes point users to `Schema`, `Endpoints`, `Lineage`, `Guides`, and the browser `Playground`.

# Why It Matters
This is the clearest single-page statement of what nbadb claims publicly: a DuckDB-first NBA warehouse with full endpoint coverage, documented lineage, and multiple user routes for analysts, operators, and explorers. It is the best top-level contract page for understanding how the project wants external users to perceive scope and maturity before they drop into lower-level docs.

# Key Excerpts
> "Star-schema NBA data warehouse documentation. DuckDB-first with full endpoint coverage, lineage, and schema docs."

> "From tip-off to final horn: Raw nba_api feeds staged in DuckDB, transformed into star-schema tables, then routed into docs pages that explain where every play and model came from."

> "Choose by question: Start with the warehouse shape, source feeds, dependency replay, or execution guides depending on what you need to answer next."

> "Three clean entries depending on whether you are scouting the model, running the pipeline, or jumping straight into analysis."

# Capture Notes
- Captured with a markdown web fetch; page shell and navigation noise were ignored in favor of the main public claims.
- The homepage is promotional but still contract-relevant because it names the core public surfaces and supported usage modes.
- Scoreboard counters are shown as docs-autogen-backed metrics rather than static marketing copy, which strengthens the page's value as a public status snapshot.
